items.title: Phase2-linux-shell: Linux Tauri shell with WebKitGTK
items.intent: Markdown runs on Linux as a first-class citizen — native file dialogs via GTK, XDG desktop integration, .desktop file, and AppStream metadata. A macOS-only editor violates D-PLAT-1.
items.description:
Create the Linux Tauri shell. The Tauri project already builds for macOS; this item extends it to produce a Linux binary using WebKitGTK. Add a .desktop file for application launcher integration, AppStream metadata for software center discoverability, and verify GTK file dialogs work correctly.

Scope boundary: this item produces a working Linux build with desktop integration. Flatpak packaging is a separate concern within this phase but can be a follow-up item if needed.
steps: Configure Tauri to build for Linux target with WebKitGTK backend
Add a .desktop file (XDG Desktop Entry) to the project with correct categories, icon, and MIME type associations for .md files
Add AppStream metadata XML for software center integration
Verify the Linux build produces a runnable binary that opens a window with the CodeMirror editor
Verify GTK file dialogs (open/save) work correctly on Linux
Test that all existing features (open, edit, save, themes, find/replace) work identically on Linux
Add Linux build target to CI

acceptanceCriteria.text: cargo tauri build --target linux produces a binary
.desktop file exists with correct MIME type for .md files

acceptanceCriteria: GTK file dialogs open and return selected file paths correctly
Editor renders and accepts input identically to the macOS build
items.groomingNotes: Fixed broken verify commands: tail pipe was swallowing build exit codes. Build verify now runs the build and checks for deb bundle. Test verify runs bare without tail.
items.lastFailReason: Baseline validation failed before developer ran

items.title: Phase2-windows-shell: Windows Tauri shell with WebView2
items.intent: Markdown runs on Windows as a first-class citizen — native file dialogs, MSI installer, and WinGet manifest. Windows is the largest desktop platform and must be supported before the first public pre-release.
items.description:
Create the Windows Tauri shell. Tauri uses WebView2 (Edge Chromium) on Windows. This item produces a Windows build with an MSI installer and a WinGet manifest for package manager distribution.

Scope boundary: this item produces a working Windows build with installer. Microsoft Store submission is a future item.
steps: Configure Tauri to build for Windows target with WebView2 backend
Configure the MSI installer bundle in tauri.conf.json (app name, icon, file associations for .md)
Create a WinGet manifest for community submission
Verify the Windows build produces a runnable .exe and .msi
Verify Windows native file dialogs work correctly
Test that all existing features work identically on Windows
Add Windows build target to CI

acceptanceCriteria.text: cargo tauri build produces an MSI installer on Windows
File association for .md is configured in tauri.conf.json

acceptanceCriteria: Windows native file dialogs open and return selected file paths correctly
Editor renders and accepts input identically to macOS and Linux builds
WinGet manifest exists and is syntactically valid
items.groomingNotes: Fixed broken verify commands: MSI check now runs build before checking for artifact. Test verify runs bare without tail. Added WinGet manifest verify.

items.title: Phase2-web-shell: Web / PWA shell with File System Access API
items.intent: Markdown runs in the browser as a progressive web app — no install required. Users can open local files via the File System Access API where supported, and fall back to file upload/download where not. A web shell makes Markdown accessible on any platform with a browser.
items.description:
Create a web build of the CodeMirror 6 editor that runs standalone in a browser without Tauri. Use the File System Access API (Chromium browsers) for native-like open/save of local files. For browsers without File System Access API (Firefox, Safari), fall back to <input type='file'> for open and download-as-file for save.

Deploy as a PWA with a service worker for offline capability and an app manifest for install-to-home-screen.

Scope boundary: this is the editor only — no Rust core in the browser (no WASM compilation of markdown-core yet). File operations are JS-only. Formatting and doctor features that depend on markdown-core are not available in the web shell until a WASM build of markdown-core is created (future item).
steps: Create a web build configuration (Vite) that produces a standalone web app without Tauri dependencies
Implement file open using File System Access API (showOpenFilePicker) with fallback to <input type='file'>
Implement file save using File System Access API (showSaveFilePicker / FileSystemWritableFileStream) with fallback to download
Add a PWA manifest (manifest.json) with app name, icons, and display: standalone
Add a service worker for offline caching of the app shell
Verify the web app loads in Chrome, Firefox, and Safari
Verify open/save works in Chrome (File System Access) and Firefox (fallback)

acceptanceCriteria.text: Web build produces a deployable static site

acceptanceCriteria: File open works in Chrome via File System Access API
File open falls back to file input in Firefox/Safari
File save works in Chrome via File System Access API
The app can be installed as a PWA from Chrome
items.groomingNotes: Fixed build verify: tail pipe was swallowing exit code. Now runs build and checks dist-web directory exists. Service worker verify made more explicit (checking .js not wildcard pattern).

items.title: Phase2-autosave: Auto-save with content-hash skip
items.intent: The user's work is saved automatically without explicit action. Auto-save only writes when the content has actually changed (content-hash skip), avoiding unnecessary disk writes and preserving file modification timestamps. Users should never lose work because they forgot to press Cmd+S.
items.description:
Implement auto-save in the Tauri app. After any document change, start a debounce timer (e.g. 2 seconds of idle). When the timer fires, compute a hash of the current document content and compare it to the hash of the last saved version. If different, save. If identical, skip.

Auto-save writes to the same file path the document was opened from. Untitled (never-saved) documents are NOT auto-saved — they require an explicit first save to establish a file path.

Auto-save is on by default. When auto-save fires, show a subtle, non-modal indicator (e.g. a brief status bar message).

Scope boundary: this item does not handle external file changes or conflicts — that is FEAT-026.
steps: Add a content hash (e.g. FNV or xxhash) computation to markdown-core or the frontend
Track the hash of the last-saved content in the editor state
Implement a debounce timer (2s idle after last change) in the frontend
On timer fire, compute current hash, compare to saved hash, save if different
Show a brief status bar indicator when auto-save completes (e.g. 'Saved' that fades after 2s)
Skip auto-save for untitled documents (no file path yet)
Ensure auto-save does not interfere with explicit Cmd+S save
acceptanceCriteria: After typing and waiting 2+ seconds, the file on disk contains the new content without pressing Cmd+S
If the user types but the content hash matches the saved version (e.g. undo back to saved state), no disk write occurs
Untitled documents are not auto-saved
A brief 'Saved' indicator appears in the status bar after each auto-save
items.groomingNotes: Fixed fragile npm build verify (was grepping for error string count — unreliable). Simplified to direct exit-code checks. Behavioral ACs intentionally lack verify commands (require interactive testing).

items.title: Phase2-file-watching: External file change detection and conflict resolution
items.intent: When an external process modifies the file the user has open (e.g. git pull, another editor, a cloud drive sync), the editor detects the change and offers a clear resolution: reload the external version, keep the editor version, or diff. The user never silently overwrites external changes or loses their own edits.
items.description:
Use the notify crate in Rust to watch the open file's path for modifications. When an external change is detected:

1. If the editor has no unsaved changes: silently reload the file.
2. If the editor has unsaved changes: show a dialog — 'This file was changed externally. Reload external version / Keep your version / Show diff.'

The file watcher should handle common edge cases: file renamed, file deleted, file replaced (atomic save pattern where the file is deleted and recreated).

Scope boundary: this item handles single-file watching. Directory-level watching for file tree updates is a future item.
steps: Add the notify crate to markdown-core dependencies
Create a file watcher module in markdown-core that watches a single file path and emits change events
Expose a Tauri command to start/stop watching a file path
In the frontend, listen for file-change events from the Tauri backend
If no unsaved changes: reload the file silently and update the editor content
If unsaved changes exist: show a native Tauri dialog with Reload / Keep / Show Diff options
Handle edge cases: file deleted (show warning), file renamed (stop watching), atomic save pattern (debounce rapid delete+create events)

acceptanceCriteria.text: notify crate is in markdown-core Cargo.toml

acceptanceCriteria: External modification of the open file with no unsaved editor changes reloads the file silently
External modification with unsaved editor changes shows a Reload/Keep/Diff dialog
File deletion while open shows a warning to the user
items.groomingNotes: Fixed stale markdown-core path references — directory is markdown-core. Fixed fragile npm build verify (was grepping error count). Behavioral ACs intentionally lack verify commands.

items.title: Phase2-large-files: Large-file handling and performance
items.intent: The editor opens and edits 10k+ line documents without degrading below the baselines established in docs/baseline.json. The document model decision made in FEAT-008 is exercised under load. If String-backed storage is insufficient, this item surfaces the evidence and implements the chosen alternative.
items.description:
Stress-test the editor with large markdown documents (10k, 50k, 100k lines). Measure open time, keystroke latency, save time, and memory footprint against docs/baseline.json thresholds.

If the current document model (String or whatever FEAT-008 chose) meets the baselines at 10k lines but degrades at larger sizes, implement optimizations: viewport-only rendering in CodeMirror (already default), lazy IPC (only send diffs rather than full content), or switch to rope/piece-table if the data supports it.

Scope boundary: this item is about measured performance, not speculative optimization. Every change must be justified by a regression against baseline.json.
steps: Create test fixtures: 10k, 50k, and 100k line markdown files with realistic content
Measure open time, keystroke latency, save time, and memory for each fixture
Compare measurements against docs/baseline.json thresholds
If any metric regresses >10% at 10k lines, investigate and fix
If metrics degrade significantly at 50k+ lines, document the ceiling and implement optimizations
Add a CI benchmark that gates on large-file performance
acceptanceCriteria: 10k-line file opens within the baseline threshold from docs/baseline.json
Keystroke latency on a 10k-line file stays within baseline threshold
Memory footprint on a 10k-line file stays within baseline threshold
Performance characteristics at 50k and 100k lines are documented
items.groomingNotes: Fixed stale markdown-core path (directory is markdown-core). Fixed fragile npm build verify and tail-swallowed test exit code. Added fixture existence check. Performance ACs intentionally lack automated verify — they require measurement against baseline.json.

items.title: Phase2-flatpak: Flatpak packaging for Linux distribution
items.intent: Markdown is installable via Flatpak on any Linux distribution. Flatpak is the most widely supported universal Linux packaging format and is required for software center visibility on GNOME, KDE, and elementary OS.
items.description:
Create a Flatpak manifest for the Markdown editor. The manifest should build from the Tauri project, bundle all dependencies (WebKitGTK runtime from the GNOME SDK), and produce an installable Flatpak.

Scope boundary: this item produces a buildable Flatpak manifest and verifies it installs and runs. Flathub submission is a separate follow-up.
steps: Create a Flatpak manifest YAML/JSON referencing the GNOME SDK and WebKitGTK runtime
Configure the manifest to build the Tauri project from source
Add .desktop file and AppStream metadata to the Flatpak build
Build the Flatpak locally and verify it installs
Verify the Flatpak-sandboxed app can open files via the XDG portal file dialog
Add Flatpak build to CI as an optional job
acceptanceCriteria: flatpak-builder produces an installable Flatpak bundle
The Flatpak-installed app opens a window and accepts editor input
File open dialog works through the XDG portal (Flatpak sandbox)

items.title: Phase3-local-ai: Local AI with on-device inference
items.intent: The user can improve, summarize, and continue writing using AI that runs entirely on their device — no account, no network, no API key. Core AI features work offline out of the box. An editor that claims AI-native but requires cloud accounts violates D-AI-1.
items.description:
Integrate on-device inference using llama.cpp (cross-platform) or MLX (macOS Apple Silicon) or ONNX Runtime. Ship or download-on-demand a small quantized model suitable for prose tasks.

First AI actions to implement:
- **Improve**: select text, invoke AI, get improved version as an inline suggestion the user can accept/reject
- **Summarize**: select text or full document, get a summary inserted at cursor or shown in a panel
- **Continue writing**: place cursor at end of content, invoke AI to generate continuation as inline ghost text

AI never modifies the document without explicit user consent (D-AI-3). Suggestions are shown inline and accepted/rejected with a single keystroke.

Model download: on first use, prompt the user to download the model (~2–4GB). Show progress. If download fails or is declined, the editor works perfectly — AI features are simply absent (DP-8).

Scope boundary: this item covers the inference runtime and the three core actions. Smart completions (continuous inline suggestions) are FEAT-032. BYO-key cloud AI is FEAT-030.
steps: Evaluate and select inference runtime: llama.cpp (via llama-cpp-rs) for cross-platform, MLX for macOS optimization
Add inference runtime as a Rust dependency in markdown-core or a new em-ai crate
Implement model download manager: check for local model, prompt to download, show progress, handle failure gracefully
Implement the improve action: take selected text + context, run inference, return improved text
Implement the summarize action: take selected text or full doc, run inference, return summary
Implement the continue action: take document up to cursor, run inference, return continuation
Expose Tauri commands for each AI action
Wire frontend: selection actions (improve, summarize) via context menu or keyboard shortcut
Wire frontend: continue writing as inline ghost text at cursor position
Show AI suggestions inline with accept (Tab) / reject (Escape) keybindings
acceptanceCriteria: Selecting text and invoking 'Improve' produces an inline suggestion that can be accepted or rejected
Selecting text and invoking 'Summarize' produces a summary
'Continue writing' at cursor position shows ghost text continuation
All AI actions work offline with no network connection
If the model is not downloaded, AI actions are absent and the editor works normally
items.groomingNotes: Fixed stale markdown-core path (directory is markdown-core). Fixed fragile npm build verify and tail-swallowed test exit code. Behavioral AI ACs intentionally lack automated verify — require interactive testing.

items.title: Phase3-byo-key: BYO-key cloud AI mode
items.intent: Power users can paste their own API key for a frontier model provider (OpenAI, Anthropic, Ollama, or any OpenAI-compatible endpoint) and get higher-quality AI results. Requests go directly from the user's machine to their provider — we never see the content or the key. There is no paid tier.
items.description:
Add a settings pane where the user can configure a cloud AI provider:
- Provider dropdown: OpenAI, Anthropic, Ollama, Custom (OpenAI-compatible endpoint)
- API key field (stored encrypted in the OS keychain via Tauri's secure storage)
- Optional: custom endpoint URL for Ollama or compatible providers
- Optional: model selection dropdown populated from the provider's model list API

When a BYO key is configured, the same AI actions (improve, summarize, continue) from FEAT-029 route to the cloud provider instead of local inference. The user can switch between local and cloud AI at any time.

Key storage: use the OS keychain (macOS Keychain, Windows Credential Manager, Linux Secret Service) via Tauri's keyring plugin. Never store keys in plain text.

Scope boundary: this item adds the settings pane and cloud routing. The AI actions themselves are defined in FEAT-029.
steps: Add a Settings UI accessible from the menu bar (Preferences on macOS, Settings on other platforms)
Create the AI provider configuration pane with provider dropdown, API key field, and endpoint URL field
Integrate Tauri's keyring/secure-storage plugin for encrypted key storage
Implement API client for OpenAI-compatible endpoints (covers OpenAI, Ollama, and custom endpoints)
Implement API client for Anthropic's Messages API
Route AI actions to cloud provider when BYO key is configured, local when not
Add a toggle to switch between local AI and cloud AI
Handle API errors gracefully: show error inline, suggest retrying or switching to local AI (DP-8)
acceptanceCriteria: Settings pane allows entering an API key for OpenAI, Anthropic, Ollama, or a custom endpoint
API key is stored in the OS keychain, not in plain text
AI actions route to the configured cloud provider when a key is set
API errors are shown inline with a suggestion to retry or switch to local AI
Removing the API key reverts AI actions to local inference

items.title: Phase3-voice-intent: Voice-intent command input
items.intent: The user can speak a command ('make this paragraph shorter', 'add a heading here', 'summarize this section') and the editor executes it as an AI action. Voice is a natural input modality for prose editing — faster than typing a prompt, more expressive than a menu item.
items.description:
Use platform speech APIs (Web Speech API in the webview, or native speech recognition via Tauri plugins) to capture voice input. Convert speech to text, interpret it as an intent (using the local or BYO-key AI model), and execute the corresponding editor action.

Activation: hold a key (e.g. Fn or a configurable shortcut) to activate voice input. Release to submit. Show a visual indicator while listening.

Scope boundary: this item implements voice-to-intent. The AI actions it triggers are defined in FEAT-029/FEAT-030. This item does not implement dictation (continuous speech-to-text for document content).
steps: Evaluate speech recognition options: Web Speech API (Chrome only), Tauri native plugin, or platform APIs
Implement voice capture with push-to-talk activation (hold key to listen, release to submit)
Show a visual indicator (microphone icon, waveform) while listening
Convert captured audio to text using the chosen speech API
Pass the transcribed text to the AI model as an intent to interpret
Map the AI's interpretation to an editor action and execute it
Handle errors: microphone permission denied, speech not recognized, AI interpretation failed
acceptanceCriteria: Holding the activation key shows a listening indicator
Speaking a command ('make this shorter') and releasing the key executes an AI action on the selected text
Microphone permission is requested on first use with a clear explanation
If speech recognition is unavailable, the feature is absent and the editor works normally

items.title: Phase3-smart-completions: Inline smart completions
items.intent: As the user types, the editor offers context-aware inline completions — ghost text that can be accepted with Tab. This is the always-on AI writing assistant: not invoked explicitly, but present as a background capability that anticipates what you're about to write.
items.description:
Implement continuous inline completions using the local AI model (FEAT-029). As the user types, after a brief debounce (e.g. 500ms of no typing), send the document context to the model and display the predicted continuation as ghost text (dimmed, not yet inserted).

Accept with Tab (inserts the ghost text). Dismiss with Escape or by continuing to type. Partial accept with Cmd+Right (accept word-by-word).

Completions must not block the editor — inference runs asynchronously and results are discarded if the user has typed further by the time they arrive.

Scope boundary: this is local-only completions. Cloud-backed completions via BYO-key would use the same UI but route through FEAT-030's API client.
steps: Implement a debounced completion trigger: 500ms after last keystroke, request a completion
Send document context (text before cursor, optionally text after) to the local AI model
Display the completion as ghost text using CodeMirror decorations (dimmed, italicized)
Accept completion with Tab (insert the ghost text at cursor)
Dismiss with Escape or by typing any character
Implement partial accept with Cmd+Right (accept one word at a time)
Cancel in-flight completion requests if the user types before the result arrives
Ensure completions do not block the editor UI thread
acceptanceCriteria: After pausing while typing, ghost text appears as a completion suggestion
Pressing Tab accepts the ghost text and inserts it into the document
Pressing Escape or typing any character dismisses the ghost text
Completions do not cause perceptible editor lag or block keystrokes

items.title: Phase4-ios: iOS Tauri mobile shell
items.intent: Markdown runs on iOS as a first-class mobile citizen — Files app integration, share sheet, swipe gestures, and UIKit sheets. The primary persona mixes desktop and mobile; cross-platform includes mobile.
items.description:
Create the iOS Tauri mobile shell using Tauri 2.0's iOS support. The shell wraps the same CodeMirror 6 editor in a WKWebView with iOS-specific integration: Files app file provider for opening .md files, share sheet for receiving .md files from other apps, and platform-idiomatic gesture support.

Scope boundary: this item produces a working iOS app that can open, edit, and save markdown files. AI features on iOS are gated on Phase 3 completing first. App Store submission is a separate follow-up.
steps: Configure Tauri for iOS target (Tauri 2.0 mobile support)
Set up Xcode project configuration (bundle ID, signing, capabilities)
Implement Files app integration via UIDocumentPickerViewController or Tauri's file dialog plugin for iOS
Add share sheet / intent filter support for receiving .md files
Verify CodeMirror 6 renders correctly in iOS WKWebView (viewport, keyboard avoidance, safe areas)
Test touch interactions: selection, scrolling, pinch-to-zoom text
Verify the on-screen keyboard does not obscure the editor content (keyboard avoidance)

acceptanceCriteria.text: cargo tauri ios build produces an iOS app bundle

acceptanceCriteria: The iOS app opens and displays the CodeMirror 6 editor
File open via Files app integration works
File save writes back to the original file location
The on-screen keyboard does not obscure the editing area
Text selection and scrolling work via touch
items.groomingNotes: Fixed tail-swallowed exit code on iOS build verify. Remaining ACs are intentionally manual (require device testing).

items.title: Phase4-android: Android Tauri mobile shell
items.intent: Markdown runs on Android — Storage Access Framework integration, intent filters for .md files, Material design patterns. Android is the largest mobile platform globally.
items.description:
Create the Android Tauri mobile shell using Tauri 2.0's Android support. The shell wraps the same CodeMirror 6 editor in an Android WebView with platform integration: Storage Access Framework for file access, intent filters for opening .md files from file managers, and Material design patterns.

Scope boundary: this item produces a working Android app. Play Store submission is a separate follow-up.
steps: Configure Tauri for Android target (Tauri 2.0 mobile support)
Set up Android project configuration (package name, signing, permissions)
Implement file access via Storage Access Framework (ACTION_OPEN_DOCUMENT, ACTION_CREATE_DOCUMENT)
Add intent filter for .md MIME type so the app appears in 'Open with' for markdown files
Verify CodeMirror 6 renders correctly in Android WebView (viewport, keyboard avoidance)
Test touch interactions: selection, scrolling, long-press context menu
Verify the on-screen keyboard does not obscure the editor content

acceptanceCriteria.text: cargo tauri android build produces an APK

acceptanceCriteria: The Android app opens and displays the CodeMirror 6 editor
File open via Storage Access Framework works
File save writes back to the original file location
The on-screen keyboard does not obscure the editing area
The app appears in 'Open with' menu for .md files
items.groomingNotes: Fixed tail-swallowed exit code on Android build verify. Remaining ACs are intentionally manual (require device/emulator testing).

items.title: Phase5-wikilinks: Wikilinks and on-demand backlinks against plain files
items.intent: The user can type [[filename]] and it resolves to a real .md file in the same directory tree. Clicking a wikilink opens the target file. Backlinks are computed on demand — not stored in a database, not requiring a vault. This is the D-NO-1 boundary: plain-file wikilinks yes, PKM database no.
items.description:
Implement wikilink syntax ([[filename]]) support in the editor:

1. **Parsing**: recognize [[...]] syntax in the editor and apply a distinct decoration
2. **Resolution**: resolve the link text to a .md file in the current file's directory tree (walk up to find the nearest match, or exact-path match)
3. **Navigation**: Cmd+click (or Ctrl+click) on a wikilink opens the target file
4. **Backlinks**: when a file is open, compute which other .md files in the directory tree link to it. Show backlinks on demand (e.g. in a panel or status bar), not persistently
5. **Creation**: if the target file doesn't exist, offer to create it

Scope boundary: no graph view, no daily notes, no tags database. Wikilinks are a file navigation feature, not a PKM system.
steps: Add wikilink syntax recognition to the CodeMirror markdown extension (custom syntax or post-parse decoration)
Implement file resolution: given a link text, find the matching .md file in the directory tree
Add Cmd+click / Ctrl+click handler on wikilink decorations to open the resolved file
Implement on-demand backlink computation: scan .md files in the directory tree for [[current-filename]] references
Add a backlinks panel or status bar section that shows backlinks on demand
Handle missing targets: offer to create the file when Cmd+clicking a wikilink to a non-existent file
acceptanceCriteria: [[filename]] syntax is visually distinguished in the editor
Cmd+clicking a wikilink opens the resolved .md file
Backlinks for the current file are computable on demand
Clicking a wikilink to a non-existent file offers to create it
items.groomingNotes: Fixed stale markdown-core path (directory is markdown-core). Fixed fragile npm and tail exit-code issues.

items.title: Phase5-extended-doctor: Extended doctor diagnostic rules
items.intent: The document doctor catches more structural and stylistic issues: passive voice, orphaned sections, inconsistent list markers. The doctor grows from a basic tool into a genuinely useful writing assistant.
items.description:
Extend the doctor engine (FEAT-011) with additional diagnostic rules:

- **Passive voice detection**: flag sentences using passive constructions, with a suggestion to rephrase
- **Orphaned sections**: detect heading-only sections with no content between them
- **Inconsistent list markers**: detect mixing of -, *, and + within the same list level
- **Long paragraphs**: flag paragraphs over a configurable word count (default 300 words) as potentially needing a break

All rules are on by default but individually disablable. Rules produce Diagnostic structs with span, severity, rule ID, and message, consistent with FEAT-011's architecture.
steps: Implement passive voice detection rule in markdown-core doctor module
Implement orphaned sections rule (heading followed immediately by another heading with no content)
Implement inconsistent list markers rule
Implement long paragraph rule with configurable threshold
Add unit tests for each new rule
Wire new rules into the doctor's rule registry
Surface diagnostics in the editor via CodeMirror lint or inline decorations
acceptanceCriteria: Passive voice constructions are flagged with a suggestion
Empty sections (heading with no content before next heading) are flagged
Mixed list markers within the same list level are flagged
Long paragraphs are flagged with the word count

items.title: Phase5-mermaid: Mermaid diagram rendering and AI-assisted editing
items.intent: Mermaid fenced code blocks render as interactive diagrams inline in the editor. AI can assist with creating and editing Mermaid syntax. AI-generated documents with diagrams render beautifully on open — this is a key claim in the press release.
items.description:
Render Mermaid diagrams inline in the editor when a fenced code block is tagged with ```mermaid. Use the Mermaid.js library to render the diagram as an SVG, displayed via a CodeMirror widget decoration that replaces the code block in rich view (or shows below it in source view).

AI-assisted Mermaid editing: when the user selects a Mermaid block and invokes an AI action, the AI can modify the Mermaid syntax and the diagram re-renders live.

Scope boundary: rendering and basic AI editing. No visual drag-and-drop diagram editor.
steps: Add mermaid.js as an npm dependency
Detect ```mermaid fenced code blocks in the CodeMirror syntax tree
Render the Mermaid syntax to SVG using mermaid.render()
Display the rendered SVG as a CodeMirror widget decoration below or replacing the code block
Re-render on content change within the mermaid block (debounced)
In rich view (The Render), replace the code block entirely with the rendered diagram
Wire AI actions to understand and modify Mermaid syntax
acceptanceCriteria: A ```mermaid code block renders as an SVG diagram in the editor
Editing the Mermaid syntax updates the diagram in real time
Invalid Mermaid syntax shows an error message instead of a broken diagram

items.title: Phase5-math: Math rendering with KaTeX
items.intent: Inline ($...$) and display ($$...$$) math expressions render beautifully in the editor. Math is a first-class content type in technical and academic markdown documents.
items.description:
Render LaTeX math expressions inline in the editor using KaTeX. Inline math ($...$) renders as styled inline elements. Display math ($$...$$) renders as centered block elements.

Use CodeMirror decorations: in source view, math delimiters are visible and the rendered output appears alongside or on hover. In rich view (The Render), delimiters are hidden and only the rendered math is shown.

Scope boundary: rendering only. No equation editor, no MathML export.
steps: Add katex as an npm dependency
Detect $...$ (inline) and $$...$$ (display) syntax in the editor
Render math expressions using katex.renderToString()
Display inline math as a CodeMirror widget decoration replacing the $...$ span
Display display math as a centered block widget decoration
Handle KaTeX errors gracefully: show the raw syntax with an error indicator
In rich view, hide delimiters and show only rendered math
acceptanceCriteria: Inline math ($x^2$) renders as a formatted math expression
Display math ($$\sum_{i=1}^n$$) renders as a centered block expression
Invalid LaTeX shows the raw syntax with an error indicator instead of crashing

items.title: Phase5-folding: Folding and outline view
items.intent: The user can fold sections of the document by heading level and navigate the document structure via an outline panel. Long documents become manageable without scrolling.
items.description:
Implement section folding and an outline/table-of-contents panel:

1. **Folding**: each heading creates a foldable region spanning to the next heading of equal or higher level. Click a gutter indicator or use a keyboard shortcut to fold/unfold. Folded regions show a placeholder (e.g. '... (N lines)').
2. **Outline panel**: a side panel showing the document's heading structure as a navigable tree. Clicking a heading in the outline scrolls to it in the editor. The outline updates live as the user edits.

Scope boundary: heading-level folding only. No arbitrary fold regions, no manual fold markers.
steps: Use CodeMirror's foldService to define fold ranges based on heading levels from the markdown syntax tree
Add fold gutters with click-to-fold indicators
Add keyboard shortcuts for fold/unfold (Cmd+Shift+[ / Cmd+Shift+])
Create an outline panel component showing the heading hierarchy
Wire outline clicks to scroll to the corresponding heading in the editor
Update the outline live on document changes
Add a toggle for showing/hiding the outline panel
acceptanceCriteria: Clicking a fold indicator in the gutter collapses the section under a heading
Folded sections show a placeholder with the number of hidden lines
The outline panel shows all headings in hierarchical order
Clicking a heading in the outline scrolls the editor to that heading

items.title: Phase5-multi-doc: Multi-document tabs and split view
items.intent: The user can open multiple documents in tabs within a single window and optionally split the view to see two documents side by side. This is the progression from the M0 one-file-per-window model to a productivity-oriented multi-document workflow.
items.description:
Add a tab bar to the editor window. Each tab holds an independent CodeMirror editor instance with its own document state. The user can:
- Open multiple files (each opens in a new tab)
- Switch between tabs with keyboard shortcuts (Cmd+Shift+] / Cmd+Shift+[)
- Close tabs (Cmd+W) with unsaved-changes prompt
- Drag tabs to reorder
- Split the view horizontally or vertically to show two tabs side by side

Scope boundary: tabs and split view. No file tree sidebar (that could be a separate item).
steps: Create a tab bar component above the editor area
Manage multiple editor instances, each with independent document state
Opening a file creates a new tab (or focuses existing tab if the file is already open)
Add tab switching shortcuts: Cmd+Shift+] (next), Cmd+Shift+[ (previous), Cmd+1-9 (by index)
Add close-tab behavior with unsaved-changes prompt
Implement drag-to-reorder for tabs
Add split view: a command to split the current view horizontally or vertically
Ensure file watching (FEAT-026) and auto-save (FEAT-025) work correctly per-tab
acceptanceCriteria: Opening multiple files creates separate tabs
Cmd+Shift+] and Cmd+Shift+[ switch between tabs
Closing a tab with unsaved changes shows a prompt
Split view shows two documents side by side

items.title: Phase5-quick-open: Quick Open fuzzy file search
items.intent: The user can press Cmd+P to fuzzy-search across all .md files in the current folder tree and open one instantly. This is the fast-switching workflow that power users expect from VS Code and Obsidian.
items.description:
Implement a Quick Open dialog triggered by Cmd+P (macOS) / Ctrl+P (other platforms). The dialog shows a text input that fuzzy-matches against all .md files in the directory tree containing the current file.

File discovery: walk the directory tree from the current file's parent directory (or a configured root), collect all .md file paths, and index them for fuzzy matching. Use a simple fuzzy matching algorithm (subsequence match with scoring for consecutive characters and path segment boundaries).

Scope boundary: file search and open. No content search (that would be a separate Cmd+Shift+F item).
steps: Implement a Tauri command that walks a directory tree and returns all .md file paths
Implement a fuzzy matching algorithm in TypeScript (subsequence match with scoring)
Create a Quick Open overlay UI: centered modal with text input and scrollable results list
Wire Cmd+P / Ctrl+P to open the Quick Open overlay
On result selection (Enter or click), open the file (in a new tab if FEAT-040 is implemented, otherwise in the current editor)
Cache the file list and refresh on focus (to catch new files)
Show the relative path of each result for disambiguation
acceptanceCriteria: Cmd+P opens a Quick Open overlay
Typing in the overlay fuzzy-matches against .md files in the directory tree
Selecting a result opens the file
Escape closes the overlay without navigation

items.title: Phase5-images: Image handling — inline rendering, drag-drop, paste
items.intent: Images in markdown (![alt](path)) render inline in the editor. Users can drag-drop or paste images and have them saved to a sensible location with the markdown reference inserted automatically. AI-generated documents with images render beautifully on open.
items.description:
Implement image handling in the editor:

1. **Inline rendering**: ![alt](path) references render the image inline as a CodeMirror widget decoration. Relative paths resolve from the current file's directory. URLs render via fetch.
2. **Drag-drop**: dragging an image file onto the editor saves it to the same directory as the current file (or a configurable images/ subdirectory) and inserts ![](relative-path) at the drop position.
3. **Paste**: pasting an image from the clipboard saves it (with a timestamp-based filename) and inserts the reference.
4. **Error handling**: broken image references show a placeholder with the alt text and path.

Scope boundary: local and URL images. No image editing, no resizing handles, no image optimization.
steps: Detect ![alt](path) syntax in the CodeMirror syntax tree
Resolve image paths relative to the current file's directory
Render images as CodeMirror widget decorations (replace the syntax with the rendered image in rich view)
Implement drag-drop handler: intercept drop events with image files, save to disk, insert markdown reference
Implement paste handler: intercept paste events with image data, save to disk, insert markdown reference
Handle broken images: show alt text and path as a placeholder
Add max-width styling so large images don't break the layout
acceptanceCriteria: ![alt](path) with a valid local image renders the image inline
Dragging an image file onto the editor inserts a markdown image reference
Pasting an image from the clipboard saves it and inserts a reference
Broken image references show a placeholder, not a broken image icon

items.title: Phase5-pdf: Render, print, and share as polished PDF
items.intent: The user can export the current document as a beautifully typeset PDF or print it directly. The PDF output should look polished — proper typography, rendered diagrams and math, styled code blocks — not a raw text dump.
items.description:
Implement PDF export and print for the current document:

1. **PDF export** (Cmd+Shift+E or File > Export as PDF): render the markdown to a styled HTML document, then convert to PDF using the webview's print-to-PDF capability or a library like Puppeteer/Playwright (headless).
2. **Print** (Cmd+P with Shift, or File > Print): open the OS print dialog with a print-formatted view of the document.

The output should include: styled headings, bold/italic, rendered code blocks with syntax highlighting, rendered Mermaid diagrams (if FEAT-037), rendered math (if FEAT-038), and images.

Scope boundary: single-document export. No batch export, no custom templates.
steps: Render the current markdown document to styled HTML (reuse The Render's decoration output or a separate render pipeline)
Add a print stylesheet that produces clean, typeset output
Implement Cmd+Shift+E: render to HTML and use the webview's print-to-PDF to save as PDF
Implement File > Print: open the OS print dialog with the styled HTML
Include rendered diagrams and math if those features are available
Add page break hints before top-level headings
acceptanceCriteria: Cmd+Shift+E exports a PDF file to the user's chosen location
The PDF includes styled headings, bold/italic, and code blocks
File > Print opens the OS print dialog with formatted content
The PDF does not contain raw markdown syntax — it is a rendered view

items.title: Phase5-custom-themes: Custom themes and fonts
items.intent: Users can customize the editor's appearance beyond the built-in light and dark themes — choose fonts, adjust colors, or install community-created themes. Personalization without a plugin system.
items.description:
Add a theme customization system:

1. **Font selection**: let the user choose body and code fonts from system-installed fonts, with a preview
2. **Color scheme customization**: let the user adjust key colors (background, text, accent, heading colors) within a theme
3. **Community themes**: support loading theme files (JSON or CSS) from a directory (~/.config/markdown/themes/)

Theme files define CSS custom property overrides. The built-in light and dark themes are the defaults; user themes override specific properties.

Scope boundary: visual customization only. No editor behavior changes via themes. No theme marketplace.
steps: Define a theme file format (JSON mapping CSS custom property names to values)
Load user themes from ~/.config/markdown/themes/ on startup
Add a Preferences > Appearance pane with font selection (body and code fonts)
Add color customization controls for key properties (background, text, accent, headings)
Apply theme overrides via CSS custom properties on the root element
Provide a theme preview in the preferences pane
Ensure custom themes work with both light and dark base themes
acceptanceCriteria: A JSON theme file in the themes directory is loaded and applied on startup
Font selection in Preferences changes the editor's body and code fonts
Color customization changes are reflected immediately in the editor
The built-in themes still work when no custom theme is present
